seoTitle: How to Add a Node in n8n: Quick Setup Guide
metaDescription: Learn how to search for, insert, and configure a new node in n8n, then connect it into your existing automation workflow.
intro: Every n8n automation is built from nodes connected together on a canvas, so knowing how to quickly find and add the right one is a core skill. This guide walks through opening the node panel, searching for the app or action you need, and wiring it into your existing workflow.

steps.heading: Open your workflow canvas
steps.body: Start from an existing or empty workflow builder canvas in your n8n dashboard.

steps.heading: Open the Add Node panel
steps.body: Click the plus icon or a connection handle on the canvas to open the node selection menu.

steps.heading: Search for the node you need
steps.body: Use the search field to filter by app integration, trigger type, or logic helper until you find the right component.

steps.heading: Drop the node onto the canvas
steps.body: Select your target node and place it on the grid where you want it in the sequence.

steps.heading: Configure its parameters
steps.body: Set up authentication credentials and choose the specific action or operation the node should perform.

steps.heading: Connect it and test
steps.body: Draw a connection line to sequence the new node with existing steps, then run a test to confirm it works as expected.

faq.question: How do I find a specific app's node quickly?
faq.answer: Use the search field inside the Add Node panel and type the app or action name to filter results instantly.

faq.question: Can I rename a node after adding it?
faq.answer: Yes, giving nodes clear custom names helps keep complex workflows organized and easier to read later.

faq.question: What if the app I need doesn't have an official node?
faq.answer: You can often still connect it using a generic HTTP Request node or by searching for a community-built node that supports it.

faq.question: Do I need to configure credentials for every node?
faq.answer: Only nodes that connect to external apps or services require credentials — logic and data helper nodes typically don't.
